header_info.title: Global Integrated Power Tracker March 2026
header_info.citation: Recommended Citation: "Global Energy Monitor, Global Integrated Power Tracker, March 2026 release."  (See the CC license for attribution requirements if sharing or adapting the data set.)
header_info.contact: Contact: James Norman, Research Analyst, Global Energy Monitor james.norman@globalenergymonitor.org

entries.name: Type
entries.definition: One of eight power technology groups (coal, oil/gas, utility-scale solar, wind, hydropower, nuclear, bioenergy, geothermal)

entries.name: Country/area
entries.definition: The Country/area column presents energy data within various economic contexts, and the geographical unit does not strictly follow political boundaries

entries.name: Subregion
entries.definition: One of 17 GEM-defined subregions of the world

entries.name: Region
entries.definition: One of five GEM-defined world regions

entries.name: Plant / Project name
entries.notes: Preference is for a government provided name. Without that information, the name used by the owner or operating company is listed

entries.name: Unit / Phase name
entries.definition: Name or number of phase in plant/project
entries.notes: Roman numerals are changed to numbers. If the owner uses non-sequential numbers or other characters, those are used

entries.name: Plant / Project name (local)
entries.definition: Commonly used name in the locally used script
entries.notes: Left blank for local languages that use the Roman/Latin alphabet. Otherwise local name(s) in local script provided if possible

entries.name: Plant / Project name (other)
entries.definition: Alternative names, including the name in local script

entries.name: Capacity (MW)
entries.definition: Total nameplate capacity, or best available data, of the project, in megawatts
entries.notes: Units: MW.

entries.name: Status
entries.definition: One of 8 status categories, see rows 112-121 below for description

entries.name: Start year
entries.definition: Year the project was or will be commissioned
entries.notes: Units: YYYY.

entries.name: Retired year
entries.definition: Year the project was or will be taken offline
entries.notes: Units: YYYY.

entries.name: Capacity Rating (solar only)
entries.definition: The reported capacity rating, unknown selected if not provided
entries.notes: Units: MWac, MWp/dc, unknown. Capacity may be reported as the DC / peak value the arrays could provide or as the AC value of what will be tied into the grid. This is often not specified and could be either value when reported as just 'MW'.

entries.name: Associated Storage
entries.definition: Value of 'yes' if a project phase has existing or planned co-located storage. Blank value otherwise. Currently data for solar and wind only.

entries.name: Fuel (combustion only)
entries.definition: See rows 185-239 below for description of fuels.

entries.name: Fuel classification (oil/gas only)
entries.definition: Fuel classification options include: Oil only, LNG only, Gas only, Multi fuel. Note "Gas only" does not include LNG.

entries.name: Hydrogen production
entries.definition: Value of 'yes' if a project phase is dedicated for green hydrogen production, 'no' if not, 'not found' or blank no data found. Only used for wind, solar, and geothermal trackers

entries.name: Hydrogen capable
entries.definition:
Hydrogen capable: turbine - The turbine at the gas plant is capable of burning some % of hydrogen. 
Hydrogen capable: progress - The gas plant has made some sort of progress beyond having a gas turbine that is hydrogen capable. This could include partnerships to secure hydrogen, announcements towards hydrogen infrastructure, or an actual hydrogen contract.
Using hydrogen - The plant is using hydrogen as a fuel.
no - The plant is not capable of using hydrogen. 
not found - no information found

entries.name: Data Center PPA (geothermal only)
entries.definition: Value of 'yes' if the unit connected to a data center through a PPA. Currently geothermal only data field.

entries.name: Country/area 1 (hydropower only)
entries.definition: Primary country/area where the project is located (unabbreviated)
entries.notes: If project is binational, Country/area 1 is either the country/area listed first in alphabetical order for projects where capacity is shared equally, or the country/area with a larger portion of the total project capacity.

entries.name: Country/area 2 (hydropower only)
entries.definition: Additional country/area where the project is located (unabbreviated)
entries.notes: If project is binational, Country/area 2 is either the country/area listed second in alphabetical order for projects where capacity is shared equally, or the country/area with a smaller portion of the total project capacity.

entries.name: Country/area 1 Capacity (MW) (hydropower only)
entries.definition: Gross capacity, or best available data, of the portion of the project which is located in Country/area 1, in megawatts, rounded to whole number
entries.notes: Units: MW. Capacity cutoffs mentioned above apply to the project as a whole, not necessarily to the portions of an international project which are within one country/area boundary.

entries.name: Country/area 2 Capacity (MW) (hydropower only)
entries.definition: Gross capacity, or best available data, of the portion of the project which is located in Country/area 2, in megawatts, rounded to whole number
entries.notes: Units: MW. Capacity cutoffs mentioned above apply to the project as a whole, not necessarily to the portions of an international project which are within one country/area boundary.

entries.name: River / Watercourse (hydropower only)
entries.definition: The name of the river or watercourse on which the project is located

entries.name: Operator(s)
entries.definition: Company that performs day-to-day operations and maintenance of the project.

entries.name: Owner(s)
entries.definition: The company that directly owns the project (as in, the lowest-level identified owner in the chain of ownership)
entries.notes: If percent ownership is known format is CompanyA [X%]; CompanyB [Y%]

entries.name: Owner(s) GEM Entity ID
entries.definition: Unique identifier assigned by the Global Energy Monitor ownership team
entries.notes: Units: E1+11 digits.

entries.name: Parent(s)
entries.definition: Highest-level project owner in the chain of ownership and ultimate parents (e.g., corporations, investment firms, and governments).
entries.notes: If percent ownership is known format is EntityA [X%]; EntityB [Y%]

entries.name: Parent(s) GEM Entity ID
entries.definition: Unique identifier assigned by the Global Energy Monitor ownership team
entries.notes: Units: E1+11 digits.

entries.name: CHP
entries.definition: Value of 'yes' if a project phase has combined heat and power/cogeneration, 'no' if not, 'not found' or blank if unknown or no data found.

entries.name: CCS
entries.definition: Value of 'yes' if a project has/plans carbon capture and storage (CCS), 'no' if not, 'not found' or blank if unknown or no data found.

entries.name: Conversion/replacement
entries.definition:
oil/gas options include: 
conversion - converts the steam turbine of an existing plant to burn gas.
replacement - existing plant is retired and replaced with a new  gas-fired plant. 
change of plans - cases in which one or more  units were planned and those plans were cancelled and subsequently gas units were planned at the same location.
use of ST in CC set - cases where there is a steam turbine (usually coal) and is then repurposed to be part of a gas-fired combined cycle unit. Can also be referred to as “repowering” into a combined cycle where gas turbine(s) and HSRGs are added to the existing steam turbine. Was labeled as "partial conversion" in previous GOGPT data releases
not found
bioenergy options include:
gas to bioenergy conversion - when a unit has been or will be converted from gas to bioenergy
coal to bioenergy conversion - when a unit has been or will be converted from coal to bioenergy
oil to bioenergy conversion - when a unit has been or will be converted from fossil oil to bioenergy
no conversion - unit is not associated with a conversion

entries.name: Unit conversion year
entries.definition: Year the unit was or is projected to be converted to bioenergy
entries.notes: Units: yyyy.

entries.name: Conversion from/replacement of (fuel)
entries.definition: Fuel that unit is converted from, or being replaced

entries.name: Conversion from/replacement of (GEM unit ID)
entries.definition: GEM Unit ID of unit that is being converted from, or being replaced
entries.notes: Units: G1+6/11 digits.

entries.name: Conversion to (fuel)
entries.definition: Fuel details connected to another GEM tracker, used when a project conversion is planned. See rows 168-222 below for description of fuels.

entries.name: Conversion to (GEM unit ID)
entries.definition: Specific ID connected to another GEM tracker, used when a project conversion is planned
entries.notes: Units: G1+11 digits.

entries.name: Captive Industry Type
entries.definition: The type of captive industry use. Options include: agriculture, aluminum, automobiles, cement & building, chemicals, coal mining & coal products, cryptocurrency, dairy, data center, electronics, hydrogen production, industrial park, iron & steel, machinery, nickel, nutrition, oil & refining, other, other metals & mining, pulp & paper, rubber, sugar, textiles, unknown.

entries.name: Captive Industry Use
entries.definition: "Captive" power plants are those that are within industrial facilities. These power plants typically supply most of their power (and if they're CHP, most of their heat as well) to the industrial facility. Options include: heat, power, both, blank.

entries.name: Captive Non Industry Use
entries.definition: Indicate if heat or power is used outside of the captive industrial facility or complex. Options include: heat, power, both, blank, None. 'None' indicates an entirely captive plant.

entries.name: Latitude
entries.definition: Latitude of the project, assumed WGS84 (Google Maps)
entries.notes: Units: decimal degrees.

entries.name: Longitude
entries.definition: Longitude of the project, assumed WGS84 (Google Maps)
entries.notes: Units: decimal degrees.

entries.name: Location accuracy
entries.definition: Approximate based on using nearest city or other information in research, or exact based on visibility on map or given in research
entries.notes: Units: approximate, exact.

entries.name: City
entries.definition: City, town, village or locality where project is located

entries.name: Local area (taluk, county)
entries.definition: County or taluk where project is located

entries.name: Major area (prefecture, district)
entries.definition: Prefecture or district where project is located

entries.name: Subnational unit (state, province)
entries.definition: Subnational unit where project is located, usually state or province

entries.name: GEM location ID
entries.definition: GEM generated ID unique for each project
entries.notes: Units: L1+11 digits. Previous GEM ID numbers have been preserved. For example, a previous GEM ID of L812345 is now L100000812345.

entries.name: GEM unit/phase ID
entries.definition: GEM generated ID unique for each phase
entries.notes: Units: G1+11 digits. Previous GEM ID numbers have been preserved. For example, a previous GEM ID of G812345 is now G100000812345.

entries.name: announced
entries.definition: Projects that have been publicly reported in corporate or governmental planning documents or media coverage but not yet actively moving forward by applying for permits or seeking land, material, or financing.

entries.name: pre-construction
entries.definition: Projects that are actively moving forward in seeking governmental approvals, land rights, or financing (the sum of 'pre-permit' and 'permitted' statuses in the Global Coal Plant Tracker)

entries.name: construction
entries.definition: Projects where site preparation and/or equipment installation are underway.

entries.name: shelved
entries.definition: Suspension of the project has been announced, or no progress has been observed for at least two years.

entries.name: shelved - inferred 2 y
entries.definition: Inferred status based on lack of observed progress for 2 years

entries.name: operating
entries.definition: The project has been formally commissioned; commercial operation has begun.

entries.name: cancelled
entries.definition: A cancellation announcement for the project has been made, or no progress has been observed for at least four years.

entries.name: cancelled - inferred 4 y
entries.definition: Inferred status based on lack of observed progress for 4 years

entries.name: mothballed
entries.definition: The project is disused, but not dismantled.

entries.name: retired
entries.definition: The project has been decommissioned or dismantled; this term is also used if the plant has been destroyed by war.

entries.name: Coal
entries.notes: Subcritical

entries.name: Coal
entries.notes: Supercritical

entries.name: Coal
entries.notes: Ultra-supercritical

entries.name: Coal
entries.notes: Circulating fluidized bed

entries.name: Coal
entries.notes: Integrated gasification combined cycle

entries.name: Coal
entries.notes: Unknown combustion technology

entries.name: Coal
entries.notes: IGCC with carbon capture and sequestration

entries.name: Coal
entries.notes: Ultra-supercritical with carbon capture and sequestration

entries.name: Coal
entries.notes: Supercritical with carbon capture and sequestration

entries.name: Coal
entries.notes: Subcritical with carbon capture and sequestration

entries.name: Coal
entries.notes: Unknown combustion technology with carbon capture and sequestration

entries.name: Oil/gas
entries.notes: internal combustion combined cycle

entries.name: Oil/gas
entries.notes: integrated solar combined cycle

entries.name: Oil/gas
entries.notes: Allam-Fetvedt Cycle

entries.name: Geothermal
entries.notes: Projects that use steam directly from a geothermal reservoir to turn a turbine.

entries.name: Geothermal
entries.notes: Projects where the high-pressure geothermal fluid is converted to steam only once.

entries.name: Geothermal
entries.notes: Projects where the remaining water after the first flash is still warm enough to flash again and used to run a second, low-pressure steam turbine.

entries.name: Geothermal
entries.notes: Such systems use three flashes to generate three different steam pressures: high, intermediate, and low. The steam flows are then used to rotate two or more turbines.

entries.name: Geothermal
entries.notes: Projects where the high-pressure geothermal fluid is converted to steam but the number of flashes is unknown.

entries.name: Geothermal
entries.notes: Projects that transfer the heat from geothermal fluid to another liquid. The heat causes the second liquid to turn to steam, which is used to drive a turbine.

entries.name: Geothermal
entries.notes: Projects that creates machine-made geothermal reservoirs in regions where there is naturally hot rock but insufficient permeability of fluid for a traditional geothermal power plant.

entries.name: Geothermal
entries.notes: Projects that use deep, large, artificial closed-loop circuits in which a working fluid is circulated and heated by sub-surface rocks through conductive heat transfer.

entries.name: Geothermal
entries.notes: Pojects that repurpose fracking technology to extract thermal energy from miles below the Earth’s surface.

entries.name: Geothermal
entries.notes: The type of geothermal power is not specified.

entries.name: Hydropower
entries.notes: The project typically or entirely generates electricity from water stored in a reservoir.

entries.name: Hydropower
entries.notes: The project generates electricity using water which is cycled between a lower and an upper reservoir by a pumping system.

entries.name: Hydropower
entries.notes: The project typically or entirely generates electricity from flowing water with little to no storage capacity.

entries.name: Hydropower
entries.notes: Technology type not specified.

entries.name: Hydropower
entries.notes: The project has both conventional storage and pumped storage capability.

entries.name: Hydropower
entries.notes: The project has both conventional storage and run-of-river capability.

entries.name: Oil/gas
entries.notes: liquefied natural gas

entries.name: Oil/gas
entries.definition: fossil gas: waste heat from natural gas

entries.name: Bioenergy
entries.notes: Biogas is composed of methane (CH4), which is the primary component of natural gas, at a relatively high percentage (50 to 75 percent), carbon dioxide (CO2), hydrogen sulfide (H2S), water vapor, and trace amounts of other gases. The energy in biogas can be used like natural gas to provide heat, generate electricity, and power cooling systems, among other uses.

entries.name: Bioenergy
entries.notes: Agricultural byproducts can be used as a fuel source. Common examples include sugar cane pulp (bagasse), rice husks, or palm kernal shells.

entries.name: Bioenergy
entries.notes: Syngas made from agricultural waste. To produce syngas, biomass is first broken down at high temperature (between 700-800°C) and high pressure in a low-oxygen environment. Under these conditions, the biomass is converted into a mixture of gases, mainly carbon monoxide, hydrogen and methane (sometimes collectively called syngas). To produce a pure stream of biomethane, this syngas is cleaned to remove any acidic and corrosive components. The methanation process then uses a catalyst to promote a reaction between the hydrogen and carbon monoxide or CO2 to produce methane. Any remaining CO2 or water is removed at the end of this process.

entries.name: Bioenergy
entries.notes: This fuel type category captures agricultural wastes whose specifics are unknown.

entries.name: Bioenergy
entries.notes: Biodiesel is a fuel manufactured domestically from vegetable oils, animal fats, or recycled restaurant grease.

entries.name: Bioenergy
entries.notes: Ethanol is a clear, colorless alcohol made from a variety of biomass materials called feedstocks. Ethanol is often made from food grains and crops with high starch and sugar content, such as corn, sorghum, barley, sugar cane, and sugar beets. Ethanol can also be made from grasses, trees, and agricultural and forestry residues such as corn cobs and stocks, rice straw, sawdust, and wood chips.

entries.name: Bioenergy
entries.notes: Paper mill wastes, sludges, waste water, and black liquor. Black liquor is a byproduct of the paper production process that can be used as a source of energy. Alkaline spent liquor is removed from digesters in the process of chemically pulping wood. After evaporation, the residual "black" liquor is burned as a fuel in a recovery furnace that permits the recovery of certain basic chemicals.

entries.name: Bioenergy
entries.notes: Landfill gas (LFG) is a byproduct of the decomposition of organic material in landfills. LFG is composed of roughly 50 percent methane (the primary component of natural gas), 50 percent carbon dioxide (CO2) and a small amount of non-methane organic compounds.

entries.name: Bioenergy
entries.notes: Refuse, also called municipal solid waste (MSW), or garbage, is combusted to harness energy at waste incinerators. MSW contains 1) biogenic (plant or animal products) materials such as paper, cardboard, food waste, grass clippings, leaves, wood, and leather products; 2) non-biomass combustible materials such as plastics and other synthetic materials made from petroleum; 3) Noncombustible materials such as glass and metals.

entries.name: Bioenergy
entries.notes: Syngas made from refuse. Syngas is produced through gasification, which uses oxygen and water vapor to produce a combustible synthesis gas (syngas) from organic compounds in the MSW, which can be used to generate electricity, produce chemicals, liquid fuels, hydrogen (H2), etc.

entries.name: Bioenergy
entries.definition: bioenergy - wastewater and sewage sludge (solids or biogas)
entries.notes: Wastewater sludge can produce biogas through a process of thermal hydrolysis and anaerobic digestion.

entries.name: Bioenergy
entries.notes: Biocoal is produced by processing dry biomass in an inert environment (no oxygen) at high temperatures, a process referred to as pyrolysis. Depending on the temperatures and the characteristics of the end product, the process may also be referred to as torrefaction. This process is commonly carried out using an indirect-fired rotary kiln.

entries.name: Bioenergy
entries.notes: Includes forestry products and byproducts, scrap lumber, lumber mill byproducts, wood chips and pellets, agricultural crops grown for energy production, etc.

entries.name: Bioenergy
entries.notes: Syngas made from wood and other biomass. To produce syngas, biomass is first broken down at high temperature (between 700-800°C) and high pressure in a low-oxygen environment. Under these conditions, the biomass is converted into a mixture of gases, mainly carbon monoxide, hydrogen and methane (sometimes collectively called syngas). To produce a pure stream of biomethane, this syngas is cleaned to remove any acidic and corrosive components. The methanation process then uses a catalyst to promote a reaction between the hydrogen and carbon monoxide or CO2 to produce methane. Any remaining CO2 or water is removed at the end of this process.

entries.name: Bioenergy
entries.notes: This category is used when the bioenergy fuel source is unknown
